Title: Book Architect: Idea to Locked Outline in One Pass
Teaser: Turn a fuzzy book idea into a complete, chapter-by-chapter outline with a single promise, a clear arc, and a word budget.
PromptHint: Generates a full non-fiction book blueprint you can write against chapter by chapter.
Prompt:
You are a veteran nonfiction book architect who has structured bestsellers. I want to write a book on [BOOK TOPIC] for [TARGET READER], whose goal is [READER TRANSFORMATION].

THINK FIRST (show this): state the ONE core promise of the book in a single sentence, name the reader's before/after states, and pick a structural spine (problem-method-proof, chronological, or framework-led). Justify the choice in 2 lines.

CONSTRAINTS:
- 8-12 chapters, each advancing the transformation; no two chapters overlap.
- Every chapter must earn its place; cut filler.
- Target total length [TARGET WORD COUNT].
- Map an emotional/logical arc (hook -> build -> payoff).

OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown):
1) Working title + 3 alternates + subtitle
2) One-sentence promise
3) Reader before/after table
4) Outline table | # | Chapter Title | Core Idea (1 line) | Reader Takeaway | Word Budget |
5) The 'spine logic' - 2 sentences on why this order delivers the promise
End with: the single chapter I should draft first and why.

Title: Chapter Drafting Engine: One On-Voice Chapter at a Time
Teaser: Draft one full chapter against your locked outline using a Role-Context-Task-Format scaffold, never the whole book at once.
PromptHint: Produces a complete, on-voice chapter draft that stays consistent with prior chapters.
Prompt:
Act as my co-author drafting ONE chapter at a time so quality never collapses.

LOCKED CONTEXT (treat as canon, do not contradict):
- Book promise & audience: [BOOK PREMISE + AUDIENCE]
- Author voice sample (mimic cadence, vocabulary, formality): [VOICE SAMPLE]
- Where we are: [PRIOR CHAPTERS SUMMARY]

TASK: Draft the chapter described here (title, core idea, and target word count): [CHAPTER SPEC: TITLE + CORE IDEA + WORD COUNT].

THINK FIRST (show as a 5-7 bullet beat sheet, then write): opening hook, 3-4 main movements, one story/example per movement, the transition that hands off to the next chapter.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Open with a scene, question, or concrete tension - never 'In this chapter we will'.
- One idea per section; vary sentence length; show, then explain.
- Stay in the supplied voice; no AI tell-words (delve, unlock, tapestry, vibrant).
- End with a 1-line bridge to the next chapter.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
First the beat sheet, then the full chapter prose with H2/H3 headings. Stop after this chapter only.

Title: Lead Magnet Forge: An Ebook That Captures Emails
Teaser: Spin one core topic into a high-converting lead magnet with a hook title, an irresistible promise, and a ready-to-write outline.
PromptHint: Designs a giveaway ebook or checklist engineered to capture emails and pre-sell your paid book.
Prompt:
You are a direct-response strategist who designs lead magnets that convert cold traffic into subscribers.

INPUT:
- Topic / expertise: [TOPIC]
- Audience & their burning problem: [AUDIENCE + PAIN]
- What I ultimately sell: [PAID OFFER]

THINK FIRST (show briefly): pick the format that best fits the pain (quick-win checklist, mini-ebook, template, or cheat-sheet) and state why in 1 line. The magnet must solve ONE specific problem fast.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Title promises a result + a timeframe.
- Deliver a genuine quick win; no fluff sections.
- Naturally bridge to the paid offer at the end (no hard sell).

OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown):
1) 5 title options (curiosity + benefit)
2) The promise (1 sentence) + who it's for / not for
3) Section-by-section outline | Section | What it delivers | Approx words |
4) Opt-in page copy: headline + 3 bullet benefits + CTA button text
5) The transition line that leads into [PAID OFFER]

Title: Self-Publishing Launch Kit: Metadata, Categories & Keywords
Teaser: Generate the Amazon KDP listing that gets your finished ebook found: categories, keywords, description, and an A+ pitch.
PromptHint: Builds discoverability metadata and a conversion-ready listing for a finished book or ebook.
Prompt:
Act as a self-publishing strategist who ranks books on Amazon KDP and other ebook stores.

INPUT:
- Book title & topic: [TITLE + TOPIC]
- Target reader & the result they want: [READER + RESULT]
- Comparable bestsellers in this niche (optional): [COMP TITLES]

THINK FIRST (show): identify the exact phrases this reader types into a store search bar, and the 2-3 sub-niches where this book can rank top-10 rather than drown in a broad category. Name them.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Keywords must be buyer search phrases, not vague topic words.
- Categories should be specific and winnable, not the most crowded ones.
- Description sells the transformation first; features second; no keyword stuffing.

OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown):
1) 7 backend keyword phrases (long-tail, search-intent)
2) 3 recommended store categories + why each is winnable (1 line each)
3) Listing description (150-200 words, scannable, benefit-led, with a hook first line)
4) 5 bullet selling points ('You'll be able to ___')
5) A 2-line A+ / above-the-fold pitch
6) The single metadata change likely to move rankings most

Title: Fiction Story Bible & Chapter-Mapped Beat Sheet
Teaser: Generate a complete story bible: premise, characters, world rules, and a chapter-mapped beat sheet.
PromptHint: Lays the structural foundation for a novel so chapters stay consistent and the arc lands.
Prompt:
You are a developmental editor and story architect for fiction.

INPUT:
- Genre & tone: [GENRE + TONE]
- One-line premise or seed: [PREMISE]
- Protagonist's want vs. need: [CHARACTER GOAL/FLAW]

THINK FIRST (show): articulate the central dramatic question and the protagonist's internal arc (lie they believe -> truth they learn). Choose a structure (3-act, save-the-cat, or hero's journey) and justify in 1 line.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Stakes must escalate; every major beat changes the protagonist's situation.
- Antagonist needs a coherent motive, not pure evil.
- World rules must be consistent and enforced.

OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown):
1) Logline (1 sentence) + central dramatic question
2) Character sheet table | Name | Role | Want | Need | Secret |
3) World rules (3-5 bullets)
4) Beat sheet table | Beat | What happens | Emotional shift | Maps to chapters |
5) The opening image and the closing mirror image
6) The first scene I should write and its goal

Title: Continuity Keeper: Never Contradict Yourself Again
Teaser: Keep a long manuscript consistent by maintaining a living 'story-so-far' canon after every chapter.
PromptHint: Outputs a compact canon summary to feed into the next chapter so the AI never contradicts itself.
Prompt:
Act as a continuity editor maintaining the canonical state of my book between chapters.

INPUT:
- The chapter I just finished (full text or summary): [CHAPTER TEXT]
- Prior running state (if any): [PRIOR STATE]
- Book type: [FICTION OR NONFICTION]

TASK: Update the running 'canon' so I can paste it into the next drafting prompt and avoid contradictions.

THINK FIRST (show): list anything introduced this chapter that future chapters must respect (facts, promises, open threads, character changes, claims I've committed to).

CONSTRAINTS:
- Be terse and high-signal; this is a reference, not prose.
- Flag unresolved threads explicitly so they get paid off.
- Note any contradiction with the prior state.

OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown):
1) One-paragraph 'story/argument so far'
2) Canon facts (bulleted) - names, terms, claims, timeline
3) Open loops to resolve | Thread | Introduced in | Must pay off by |
4) Voice/tense/POV reminders
5) Contradiction warnings (or 'none')
6) What chapter [NEXT CHAPTER] must accomplish next

Title: First-Page Surgeon: Hook Them in 30 Seconds
Teaser: Rewrite a weak chapter or book opening into a page-one hook readers can't put down.
PromptHint: Diagnoses a flat opening and returns three sharper openings in your voice.
Prompt:
You are a ruthless opening-line editor who knows readers decide in the first 30 seconds.

INPUT:
- Current opening (paste it): [DRAFT OPENING]
- Book type & vibe: [GENRE/NONFICTION + TONE]
- The reader I must grab: [TARGET READER]

THINK FIRST (show): diagnose why the current opening is slow - does it warm up, over-explain, bury the tension, or start too abstract? Name the single biggest problem.

CONSTRAINTS:
- An opening must do ONE of: drop us into a scene, pose an urgent question, state a surprising claim, or create a knowledge gap.
- No throat-clearing ('In today's world', 'Have you ever wondered').
- Keep the author's voice; sharpen, don't replace.

OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown):
1) Diagnosis (2-3 bullets)
2) Three rewritten openings, each labeled with its technique (scene / question / claim / gap)
3) Why each would hook [TARGET READER]
4) My pick + a one-line reason

Title: Glossary & Deep-Dive Builder for Any Manuscript
Teaser: Produce a clean A-Z glossary or a deep-dive concept explainer that slots straight into your manuscript.
PromptHint: Generates publishable reference sections with a fixed structure and word budgets.
Prompt:
Act as a technical writer who produces crisp, beginner-clear reference sections for books.

INPUT:
- Book subject & reader level: [SUBJECT + LEVEL]
- Terms or concept to cover: [TERMS OR CONCEPT]
- Mode: GLOSSARY or DEEP-DIVE: [MODE]

THINK FIRST (show in 1-2 lines): name the reader's likely misconceptions about this material, and the single analogy domain (cooking, sports, money, etc.) that will make it click for this audience. You'll lean on it below.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Plain language; define jargon the first time it appears.
- Use a concrete analogy or example for anything abstract.
- Stay on the supplied reader level - don't over- or under-explain.

OUTPUT FORMAT:
If GLOSSARY (~800 words): alphabetized list of 20-25 entries | Term | 1-2 sentence definition | Example in use |
If DEEP-DIVE (~900 words) on one concept, use these headings in order: Simple definition -> Detailed breakdown -> Why it matters -> How it works in practice -> Common misunderstandings -> Advanced notes. Use analogies liberally and end with a 1-sentence recap.

Title: Back-Cover Blurb That Turns Browsers Into Buyers
Teaser: Write the back-cover description and store sales copy that makes browsers buy.
PromptHint: Turns your book's premise into a benefit-led blurb, bullets, and a hook headline.
Prompt:
You are a book marketing copywriter who writes back covers that convert browsers into buyers.

INPUT:
- Book title & topic: [TITLE + TOPIC]
- Who it's for & their pain: [READER + PAIN]
- What they'll be able to do after reading: [PAYOFF]
- Author credibility (optional): [AUTHOR BIO]

THINK FIRST (show briefly): identify the reader's #1 desire and the objection stopping them from buying.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Lead with the reader's problem, not the book's features.
- Sell the transformation; benefits over contents.
- Tone matches the book; no overpromising or fake hype.

OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown):
1) 3 hook headlines (curiosity / pain / aspiration)
2) Back-cover blurb (120-160 words)
3) 4-5 benefit bullets ('You'll learn how to ___')
4) One-line 'this book is for you if...' / 'not for you if...'
5) A short author-credibility line (if bio given)
6) Closing CTA sentence

Title: De-Slop Pass: Make Any Draft Sound Human
Teaser: A final-stage line edit that strips AI tells and rewrites a draft to read like a real human author.
PromptHint: Rewrites any chapter for natural cadence and specificity while preserving meaning.
Prompt:
Act as a line editor whose only job is to make a draft sound human, not machine-generated.

INPUT:
- Draft to fix (paste): [DRAFT TEXT]
- Target voice (formal / conversational / witty / authoritative): [VOICE]
- Author's personal flavor or a sample line (optional): [VOICE SAMPLE]

THINK FIRST (show as a short list): flag the AI tells present - banned words (delve, embark, unlock, revolutionize, vibrant, tapestry, realm), transition scaffolding ('Moreover', 'In conclusion'), robotic parallelism, and vague abstractions.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Preserve the original meaning and key facts exactly.
- Vary sentence length; let some be short. Punchy.
- Inject specificity (numbers, proper nouns, one concrete detail) where it's generic.
- Cut cliche openers and filler; no em-dash overuse.

OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown):
1) Tells found (bulleted)
2) The rewritten passage (clean, ready to paste)
3) 2-3 notes on what changed and why it reads more human

Title: Chapter Workbook Builder: Turn Readers Into Doers
Teaser: Generate reflection prompts, action steps, and exercises that turn passive readers into doers.
PromptHint: Adds an end-of-chapter workbook section so the book actually changes reader behavior.
Prompt:
You are a curriculum designer who builds end-of-chapter exercises that make a book actually work for readers.

INPUT:
- Chapter topic & key lesson: [CHAPTER TOPIC + LESSON]
- Reader & their context: [READER + CONTEXT]
- Desired action after the chapter: [DESIRED ACTION]

THINK FIRST (show): name the gap between knowing and doing for this lesson - what stops readers from applying it?

CONSTRAINTS:
- Exercises must be doable in under 20 minutes.
- Move from reflection -> application -> commitment.
- Concrete and specific to this chapter's lesson, not generic journaling.

OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown):
1) 2 reflection questions (surface the reader's current reality)
2) 1 step-by-step action exercise (numbered, with a clear deliverable)
3) 1 fill-in-the-blank commitment ('This week I will ___ by ___')
4) A self-check: 'You've nailed this chapter if you can ___'
5) Optional stretch challenge for advanced readers

Title: Title & Subtitle Generator That Sells the Click
Teaser: Brainstorm magnetic book titles and subtitles, then pressure-test the top picks against your reader.
PromptHint: Produces ranked title/subtitle pairs plus a memorable series name option.
Prompt:
Act as a book-naming strategist who has titled category bestsellers.

INPUT:
- What the book is about: [BOOK TOPIC]
- Target reader & the result they want: [READER + RESULT]
- Vibe (punchy / authoritative / playful / curious): [DESIRED VIBE]
- Comparable titles I like (optional): [COMP TITLES]

THINK FIRST (show): state the emotional hook and the keyword a buyer would search. A great title is short, evocative, and hints at transformation; the subtitle clarifies the promise and audience.

CONSTRAINTS:
- Main titles <= 5 words where possible; memorable and speakable.
- Each subtitle must name the benefit and/or the reader.
- No generic words ('Guide', 'Handbook') unless they sharpen clarity.

OUTPUT FORMAT (Markdown):
1) 12 title + subtitle pairs grouped by angle (benefit / curiosity / metaphor / contrarian)
2) Top 3 picks with a one-line rationale each
3) 1 series name (if this could become a set of books)
4) The single title I'd test first and why
